description: HTTP/2 CONNECT internal port scan (PortSwigger Top 10 / 2025, #9 — flomb). HTTP/2 :method CONNECT with :authority targeting internal hosts/ports can establish raw tunnels through the front-end proxy. When the upstream allows arbitrary CONNECT, an attacker maps the internal network. Behaviour-based: TCP-open hosts respond differently from TCP-closed (timing or status), exposing the topology.

h2_connect_localhost.description: Front-end H2 server allows :method=CONNECT with :authority=127.0.0.1:<port> — direct internal port probe.

probes.description: H2 CONNECT to 127.0.0.1:22 — observe whether tunnel established (status 200 + persistent TCP) or rejected. Compare timing vs unreachable port (e.g. 127.0.0.1:1).
H2 CONNECT to internal RFC1918 host (10.0.0.1:80 / 172.16.0.1:80 / 192.168.0.1:80) — port-scan internal network from the front-end's vantage.

h2_connect_cloud_metadata.description: H2 CONNECT to cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254 AWS/Azure, metadata.google.internal GCP). When the front-end is hosted in a managed cloud, CONNECT may grant raw access to IMDSv1.

probes.description: H2 CONNECT to 169.254.169.254:80 — IMDS reach test. Pair with subsequent GET /latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ over the tunnel.

h2_connect_admin_iface.description: H2 CONNECT to internal admin interfaces (Consul 8500, Vault 8200, etcd 2379, Kubelet 10250). Front-end ACL bypass when CONNECT bypasses path-based routing.

probes.description: H2 CONNECT to 127.0.0.1:8500 (Consul) / 8200 (Vault) / 2379 (etcd) / 10250 (Kubelet). Status delta vs closed port confirms reachability.
